behaviors.description: An image containing explicit content is flagged and held for review, not published.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A pure in-process mock of the vision model could miss the real failure if the flagging decision doesn't actually prevent publication. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the publish service to accept the flag would hide failures in the actual publish-service integration (e.g., flag not propagated, database doesn't persist flag, race condition drops the item). Real failure boundary: the flagging decision must actually affect downstream publishing behavior across service boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a test image to the intake service, mock the vision model to classify as EXPLICIT_CONTENT, then verify: (1) the post enters the 'held_for_review' state in the database, (2) the post does NOT appear in the published feed, (3) the post is visible in the moderation queue for human review.

behaviors.description: A text post containing hate speech is flagged regardless of obfuscation techniques (e.g., letter substitution, unicode lookalikes).
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: YES. Correctness depends on what the real text model produces. MOCK-MISS: A mock that always returns HATE_SPEECH for any input would hide the real failure—that the model fails to detect obfuscated variants (e.g., 'h4t3 sp33ch'). MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the text model masks the core capability gap. The failure is not in the application logic, it's in the model's ability to handle obfuscation.
behaviors.test_description: Submit text posts with hate speech in multiple obfuscated forms (character substitution, unicode lookalikes, phonetic replacements, etc.) to the real text model via the moderation pipeline. Verify each variant is flagged. Repeat 2–3 times to check consistency. Track which obfuscation techniques are missed (if any) and document in failure analysis.

behaviors.description: A post that passes both content checks is published rather than held or silently dropped.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Mocking both vision and text models leaves out critical integration points. Real failures: (1) post gets lost between the safety checks and the publish step (e.g., database write failure, queue corruption), (2) the publish step itself is broken, (3) race condition causes the post to be dropped. The failure boundary is whether the safe post actually makes it to the published feed—not just whether the logic is correct, but whether the system state changes correctly end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a post with safe image and safe text, mock both models to return SAFE, then verify: (1) post enters 'approved' state in database, (2) post appears in the published feed within 100ms, (3) post is NOT in review queue, (4) confirmation is returned to the client before publish completes (if async).

behaviors.description: The full moderation pipeline—image check, text check, publish decision—completes within 5 seconds of submission.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: YES. Latency depends on real network calls to vision and text models, real database writes, real message queue operations. MOCK-HIDE: Using in-memory mocks hides the real bottleneck—actual LLM inference latency dominates in production. The 5-second SLA failure is a performance failure that can only be caught with real or representative infrastructure (actual model calls or load-test fixtures that simulate their latency).
behaviors.test_description: Submit end-to-end moderation requests with real or load-representative image/text payloads through the full pipeline (intake → vision model → text model → publish). Measure wall-clock latency from submission to decision. Run 100+ requests and verify: (1) median latency < 5s, (2) p99 latency < 5s, (3) no requests timeout or fail due to slow processing. Identify and measure the latency of each stage (image upload, vision inference, text inference, database write, publish).

behaviors.description: When the AI vision model is unavailable, image posts are queued for retry rather than auto-approved.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: YES. Requires testing real network failure (connection refused, timeout, 500 error). MOCK-MISS: A mock that returns an error response might not catch real failures like (1) retry queue doesn't actually exist or is misconfigured, (2) the fallback logic auto-approves instead of queuing, (3) queued items are lost on crash because retry queue isn't persisted, (4) timeout logic is too aggressive and doesn't retry. The failure boundary is whether the system gracefully handles unavailability—this requires simulating actual unavailability (not a mocked error response).
behaviors.test_description: Stop or block the vision model service (kill the container, firewall the port, etc.), submit image posts to the moderation pipeline, verify: (1) image posts are NOT auto-approved, (2) image posts are persisted in a retry queue, (3) retry queue survives a crash and restart of the moderation service, (4) posts are retried when vision model comes back online. Check retry logs for backoff/exponential retry behavior.

behaviors.description: The moderation decision for a borderline image is consistent when the same image is submitted multiple times within a short window.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: YES. LLM outputs are inherently non-deterministic; consistency depends on what the real vision model produces. MOCK-MISS: A deterministic mock hides the real failure—that the model itself is non-deterministic and produces different decisions for the same borderline image. This is not an application logic failure, it's a model behavior failure. The test must call the real model multiple times and measure variance.
behaviors.test_description: Select or create 3–5 borderline images (edge cases: partially visible explicit content, unclear context, ambiguous symbols). Submit each image 5 times to the real vision model within 30 seconds (to test within-window consistency). Verify: (1) each image receives identical decisions across all 5 submissions, (2) decisions match expected labels (manually vetted ground truth), (3) any inconsistencies are logged with model output details. If inconsistencies detected, document prevalence and consider whether consistency is critical SLA or expected model behavior.
